item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Eh. Format screw just seems lame (neither significant nor subtle), and there are too many references I don't get. Everything seems to be a hodgepodge of stuff, and doesn't make a decent story or article in general.

Downvoted.

item.title: Staff Post - Wikiwalk Proposal
item.content:encoded:
Timer Expired. Edits made as discussed below. Author may revert at any time.

The WikiWalk team is proposing edits to this page. All proposed edits will be in a collapsible below this post, and after 7 days if there are no serious objections to the edits, the feedback received will be taken into account as the edits are made. If you have questions not directly related to the article about this project, please pm a member of the WikiWalk Team

+ Show Block
- Hide Block

Chubert wrote a pretty cool tale featuring SCP-2156 entitled Exploded Diagram of a Young Woman which can easily in-line linked into this article, and not provide the reader with a little more detail on the skip, but also better highlight a very solid tale. As such, I am recommending the following edits:

Changing this:

Reports indicate that SCP-2156 began to develop insomnia during January of 2014. Poor grades and dismissal from its part-time job (both due primarily to symptoms of insomnia) resulted in the development of substantial anxiety. No immediate trigger for the development of SCP-2156's anomalous properties has been found.

To this:

Reports indicate that SCP-2156 began to develop insomnia during January of 2014. Poor grades and dismissal from its part-time job (both due primarily to symptoms of insomnia) resulted in the development of substantial anxiety. No immediate trigger for the development of SCP-2156's anomalous properties has been found.

If anyone has other/better examples that could be used here please leave a comment saying so!

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: After checking out chubert's Exploded Diagram of a Young Woman I was compelled to reread this, tried to upvote, and found I'd already done so. As someone who finds shuffling cards and tooling around with them ridiculously relaxing, this hits pretty unnervingly close to home — though arguably the fact that she considers her solitaire fugue states as just a game of Klondike on a crappy Windows computer might've done just as much to secure my upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The classification you used makes sense. Personally, I refer to stuff the Foundation contains as "anomalous instances" as they are anomalous (break rules of physics, biology, psychology, or any number of other accepted sciences) and are instances of such anomalies (objects, places, events, creatures, people, actions, letters, et cetera)

I'm okay with it. Honestly, I think it kind of makes sense in cannon that some enterprising researcher would make something like that to clarify the documentation, but it doesn't catch on because the Foundation has some many articles that it would be impossible for the researcher's chagnes to be implemented overall, so only a few articles show signs of these changes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sex is gross. Nobody has sex in my SCPs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: This probably seems somewhat immature of me, but how many times has one of SCP-2156's "visitors" involved a conjugal visit? Perhaps it would help with the insomnia and anxiety? She is a college student, after all…

item.title: Y'know
item.content:encoded: For a skip called Red and Black written by Scanny, this had surprisingly low amounts of communism and anarchy.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
First, if (as in your headcanon) numerology can have a triggering or strengthening effect on reality benders (psychologically likely enough), SOP would have run up a list of Klondike-derived SCP # assignations to be avoided. No sane researcher (and, yes, they aren't all sane) is going to risk the upscale that might happen when her official name is a numeralogical link in the chain! Imagine Earth's layers swapping!

I don't think the Foundation really understands how numerology functions — or at least, it doesn't in the kind of canon this runs under.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I like this one, but it stops just shy of a +1 for a few minor reasons.
First, if (as in your headcanon) numerology can have a triggering or strengthening effect on reality benders (psychologically likely enough), SOP would have run up a list of Klondike-derived SCP # assignations to be avoided. No sane researcher (and, yes, they aren't all sane) is going to risk the upscale that might happen when her official name is a numeralogical link in the chain! Imagine Earth's layers swapping!
Second, for better reasons than "Change is bad" or "It looks wrong," I've got problems with the new format. Now, as has been mentioned, an organization like the Foundation (in setting or as IRL writing project) has creative loose cannons and headcanons that would certainly try idiosyncratic or "better" classification systems, so it's presence in the lists doesn't bother me at all. However, as a new and accepted system, it has a big problem. Bureaucracies (like the Foundation) do trend toward terminological complexity, but that is a dangerous trend that smart organizers tend to oppose. The One-word system has flaws, but doesn't, in itself, allow drift into more complex systems. Right now, you can look at it and know weather you need to be mortally afraid (Keter) or just existentially afraid (most everything else). Your 2-word system is fine, and only minimally slows recognition of danger-class, but opens what, in setting, could be a slippery slope toward 4 word or 7 word classifications. That kind of detail would also invite more complex formal terminology (just think about arguments on what constitutes Keter) until it was too dense for immediate perusal. So, not a condemnation of your writing, more an admission that, were I to like in the SCPverse, I would probably become an over-literal hidebound neurotic with a stick up their rectum - all for sound reasons of safety.

Um, so that was it. Logistical concerns over classification and risky numerology - all in-setting. The ideas were fun and well delivered.

item.title: Re: eyes, cosplay, and the meaning of life
item.content:encoded: If she's going with red skin, red eyes would probably be the most accurate. Truth be told, I never really thought about eye color when writing this.

item.title: Re: eyes, cosplay, and the meaning of life
item.content:encoded: Red or black eyes, personally. Maybe white.

item.title: eyes, cosplay, and the meaning of life
item.content:encoded: Does anyone have a headcanon about what her eye color may be? For some reason I keep picturing yellow, but maybe that's dumb. I'm cosplaying her for Halloween, is why, and I'm considering getting special effects/costume contact lenses. (I'm doing red skin, in case you are wondering.)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Overall, I really like it. All of the quibbling over the class definition is indeed a bit tiresome. Working with archival filing, I cannot count the number of times someone adds a new classification mid-stream. The foundation is a HUGE organization, and people tend to do things the way that makes sense to them. More people: more odd entries.

In the 'real' world, one of two things will happen: either oversight will reclassify the item (strikethrough with the new class next to it), or the new class begins getting applied to older documentation that fits the new class.

No catalogue is going to cover every possible entry from the get-go; things will always need to be added. Especially here. We have gone from having a few hundred SCPS to nearly two thousand. I think staff need to look at expansion/recategorization strategies, not just stomp this idea because it's "different."

+1 for one of the most unique greens I've seen in awhile.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Very vaguely relevant.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The object class doesn't bother me as much as is seems to be bothering other people. I enjoyed your SCP. I like the solitaire theme combined with the concept of someone's mental disorders manifesting themselves as anomalies.

+1

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
You know, the more I think about this article, the more the object class bothers me, and I've figured out why. Two reasons:

1. It negates the fundamental conceit of the site. No matter one's headcanon, I think we all agree at the very least that the Foundation is an organization that contains and meticulously catalogues things. That's the central idea from which everything else is derived. Well, there is one quality that is essential to the efficient functioning of an organization like this, and that is standardization. No similar organization would let employees format reports any way they want let alone categorize things according to personal whims rather than the official established criteria. It's like a biologist just deciding he's going to write the species before the genus from now on.

2. It does this for no reason. It adds nothing of value. If it did, it could perhaps make sense to propose a change for the community to decide for or against, but as it stands, as far as I can see, it doesn't impart more than it takes away.

So, in the spirit of collaboration and community, there's my take on the article. That is all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I personally think it's kind of ridiculous how people are expected to be restricted by a certain formula. >_>

This is a writing site, it doesn't hurt to be creative…

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Check yourself before you Shrek yourself, Scan.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Concur.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Downvote for

Anomaly Class: Euclid // Object

The foundation should have a standard formula, with the exceptions being scips that cause a distortion in their descriptions themselves.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The common meaning of object involves something tangible. By that definition, a lot of anomalies the Foundation studies are not objects. They may be events, for example. There are meanings of object that do not mean tangible things, but none of them really apply to the usage we use. So yes, Anomaly Class is, technically, more correct than Object Class in many cases, though we usually use Object Class because, well, that's how we've always done it. Same reason people still use amnesiacs instead of amnestics.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Okay, you've lost me. We're clearly having two completely different conversations, because precisely none of this is relevant to the issue at hand. Or in touch with reality. I'm out, have fun not liking the new terminology.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: But they're still objects, because we call anomalies objects. There's sort of an inherent refusal to accept that things are actual anomalies. Compare the Foundation's approach to them versus other GoIs.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No, Wilson means that some SCPs are not objects in the literal sense. See: SCP-990, SCP-1422, SCP-304, many others.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: But that's like… an entire driving point behind the Foundation. Humanoids especially are called objects to prevent there being too much sympathy between the researchers and the skip.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I definitely wouldn't mind a well written interview. The whole numerology thing and the explanation of your headcanon pushed me to a +1 as well, though I would like to see some hint of that headcanon in the article itself if at all possible.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: next up: replace an entire article with dubstep

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think the changing of the Object Class to Anomaly Class is actually a good idea, since some of the "objects" are not, strictly speaking, objects at all.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I liked the references, I like the human-with-mental-health-problems, I like the style. I didn't notice the object class change, but I like it having gone back and looked again. Keep it up, Scantron. This site is too damned set in its ways as it is.

item.title: Re: Welcome to Scantron's coldpost theater.
item.content:encoded: Understood.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I suggest you to go back on the // Object thing, people tend to like it simple and reject the idea for minor reasons.

This is right on multiple levels ;)

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I've never really given two shits about standard format (seriously guys, he added one word and two punctuation marks), and this tickles most of the right spots with me.

item.title: Re: Welcome to Scantron's coldpost theater.
item.content:encoded:
If you're not down with SCPs written to get attention, you can go downvote all of my other articles too.

E: Because it could be misinterpreted — you're free to downvote for the anomaly class, but votes should not be made based on anything but the content of the article.

item.title: Re: Welcome to Scantron's coldpost theater.
item.content:encoded: Sorry Scantorn, but I just can't accept "anomaly" and //object thing ,and the whole wanting attention. Downvote

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I think my problem with this is a certain disconnect between the idea (person who is solitaire) and the effects, aside from the shuffling items bit. It's just not doing anything for me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Sorta. They're playing it with their surroundings.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
So, it's a person that sometimes plays solitaire with the world around it.

Wow, that is incredibly boring.

Also, format screw on a completely mundane article. If there's a format screw, it would be nice if there were a good reason for it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: It seems reasonable to me that many, if not most, of the humanoids contained have psychological issues, and depression is perhaps the most common response. If whatever happened to make them anomalous didn't cause issues, the way the Foundation treats them almost certainly would.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Eh, yes? If I'm understanding this correctly, the person is a living solitaire windows game, right?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Because in this system, "Object" encompasses anything that you can move to where you need it and put it in some sort of "box", whether that box is a concrete cube, a containment locker, a hollowed out boulder surrounded by chanting priests, or a Type 5 Humanoid Containment Cell. It states that the containment procedures fit this overall schema of the Foundation having control over the anomaly and its location. The information that it's shaped like a human is simply not exceptionally important.

As for the rest, that's kind of the point of the article, so I'm not sure how much I can help you there. Have you ever played Solitaire before?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
@HomicidalHotdog: I wanted something that looked like it could be put as a standard template for any SCP object, with the details filled in. And besides, the idea wouldn't get across as clearly with a "Phenomenon Class" as it would with an "Anomaly Class: Phenomenon".

@TheShrike: A semicolon is a possibility. I will consider it. The double slashes just sorta felt right to me.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Ah, okay, I get it now. Changed to "52nd".

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: The problem is, 'fifty-second active state' reads as 'active state lasting fifty seconds' rather than '52nd active state'.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I actually had the same problem. It is not aesthetically pleasing to me. Why double slashes? And why does "Object" come second?

Like, at least "Object; Euclid" would make more sense, I think.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Anomaly Class: Euclid // Object

I'm not hating on the Anomaly Class, I just don't understand why it's, "Euclid // Object." Wouldn't it be "Anomaly Class: Euclid // Humanoid" or something?

Also, you kind of lost me on the Window 7 operating system and the card game business. The red and the black theme, along with all the other details revealed as it goes on, just seem a bit, patchwork to me? :/

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
re: the classification system.

Rather than changing to "anomaly class" and sort of awkwardly tacking on "/ / object" why not simply stick with "__ class:" and then put "object" or "location" in that blank space? Obviously it would retain the standard format in this article, but any time you want to do a location you would simply use that. The format of your alternate system is just… ugly, I guess.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Seconded.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Let's look at the pros and cons.

Pro: Sensible, understandable, alternate classification system.

Pro: Not-horrible humanoid, all around good writing.

Con: It's both boring and forgettable.

No vote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: Thanks, fixed.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
"The positions of items not fully contiguous with SCP-2156's surroundings will be displaced at high velocity, often causing damage to SCP-2156's surroundings. Often, stacks between two and seven items high are briefly formed."

I would suggest changing the second 'often' to something else, such as 'commonly', or 'occasionally'. Other than that, +1.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
The more I think about it, the more I agree with the idea of an interview working here. I was considering it during writing, but at the time I only have slight inklings of what would go into one.

Might also be a good place to shore up some minor plot holes.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
A reworking certainly isn't out of the picture, but at the very least I'd need sleep.

And I totally get the tiredness of humanoid SCPs with mental health issues, although in this case, it's strictly relevant to the development and manifestation of its anomalous properties. Otherwise, I wouldn't have bothered with mentioning it.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Unless either the girl or someone in the Foundation who was in charge of her intake process is a numerologist, I'm not sure you can do much toward it. That's the trouble: to include it in the article meaningfully, the Foundation needs to know it somehow…

Brief interview with the Skip about how this happened from her perspective? Could be a good opportunity to make her look a little unhinged by all of it, and asserting the voracity of numerology could even help that. Dunno.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I figured the number itself had something to do with it. I wouldn't have caught that however…never counted the number of cards in play when I play.

And as for the other hook…yikes. That's…not an appealing thought, and I now see why they make sure she's not in an active state when they go in and clean things up. Me gusta, and a well deserved upvote.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
Eh. It's not bad, but it's just not clicking with me in terms of interest. Some of it has to do more with site-wide trends (it stands to reason that humanoids in containment will have mental health issues, but it seems lately that there's been a parade of depressed humanoids), but there's just too many little moving parts on this one to feel like a smooth narrative. I would reconsider my vote if you reworked this a bit.

FWIW, I couldn't care less about the format tweak here, but from a pragmatic standpoint, I think it's proving distracting to many readers out of proportion to the value it adds to the article.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: No. It vanishes every fifty-second active state. The other fifty-one states don't make it disappear. Should I try to clarify that?

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
SCP-2156 will involuntarily enter an active state for roughly one minute between three and seven times daily.

After every fifty-second active state, SCP-2156 will disappear completely for thirteen hours and four minutes before reappearing at its prior location.

Don't these contradict each other? If the SCP vanishes for 13 hours every time it has an active state, it can't squeeze in more than two each day.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
I've made an edit that might highlight the intended hook.

Hint:
People are like onions. They have layers.

Okay, it's cheap implied body horror. But in my defense, I didn't put all of my eggs in that basket.

e: And I snuck in some 28s (1+2+3+4+5+6+7, the number of cards on the board at the beginning of a game of Klondike solitaire). 2156 is 28*77. 13h04m is 784 minutes, or 282.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded: I kind of like this format, more exact and scientific in my opinion. Maybe one day this can be the normal format.

item.title: (no title)
item.content:encoded:
It's not bad, but I don't really see a point beyond "lolcards". Neutralvote.

As a side note, I couldn't give a crap about the nonstandard object class, and feel its inclusion is independent of the merits of the article.
